name: Bit Manipulation
coreIntuition: Bit manipulation operates on the binary representation of numbers. The key insight: **each bit is an independent boolean variable**. XOR finds differences, AND finds commonalities, OR combines. When a problem involves pairs, toggles, or power-of-2 properties, bits are the natural language.
mentalModel: Think of each integer as a row of switches (bits), each ON or OFF. XOR is 'toggle if different', AND is 'both must be ON', OR is 'either can be ON'. The power of bit manipulation: operations on ALL 32/64 bits happen simultaneously in O(1). It's free parallelism.
whenToUse: The problem involves XOR properties (find the unique element, find missing number)
You need to check or manipulate specific bits (powers of 2, even/odd)
The problem involves subsets (each subset maps to a bitmask)
You need to optimize a problem where each element has two states (include/exclude)
The problem involves toggling or pairing

thinkingFramework.detail: XOR of a number with itself = 0. XOR of a number with 0 = itself. AND with 1 extracts a bit. These properties are the building blocks.
Many problems decompose: compute the answer for each bit position independently, then combine. 'Sum of XOR of all pairs' → for each bit, count how many numbers have it set.
Check bit i: (n >> i) & 1. Set bit i: n | (1 << i). Clear bit i: n & ~(1 << i). Toggle bit i: n ^ (1 << i). Lowest set bit: n & (-n). Clear lowest set bit: n & (n-1).
An n-bit integer represents a subset of n elements. Bit i set → element i is included. Enumerate all subsets: for mask in 0 to 2^n - 1.

commonTraps.trap: Integer overflow or sign issues with bit shifts
commonTraps.howToAvoid: Use unsigned types or be careful with the sign bit. In Java, use >>> for unsigned right shift. In Python, integers have arbitrary precision, so less of an issue.

commonTraps.trap: Forgetting that XOR is its own inverse
commonTraps.howToAvoid: a ^ b ^ b = a. This means XOR can 'undo' itself — useful for finding single unique elements, in-place swapping, and encryption.

commonTraps.trap: Using bitmask DP when n is too large
commonTraps.howToAvoid: Bitmask DP is O(2^n × n). Only practical when n ≤ 20 or so. Don't try bitmasks with n = 100.

interviewVariantStrategy: Bit manipulation interview problems: (1) **Single number** — XOR all elements to find the unique one. (2) **Counting bits** — Brian Kernighan's algorithm (n & (n-1) removes lowest set bit). (3) **Power of 2** — n & (n-1) == 0 for powers of 2. (4) **Subsets** — bitmask enumeration. (5) **XOR queries** — prefix XOR for range XOR queries. The interview twist: problems often don't mention bits directly. 'Find the missing number' → XOR. 'Find two non-repeating elements' → XOR + splitting by a distinguishing bit. Recognize that pairing/cancellation suggests XOR.

resources.title: Errichto — Bit Manipulation for CP
resources.why: Comprehensive and practical — covers all essential bit tricks used in competitive programming and interviews

resources.title: Bit Twiddling Hacks — Stanford
resources.why: The definitive reference for clever bit manipulation tricks — useful for optimization and understanding

resources.title: LeetCode — Bit Manipulation Problems
resources.why: Curated problems that test bit manipulation understanding from easy to hard
